Due to ethical restrictions imposed by the University of Pennsylvania Institutional Review Board, patient and human subject data are available upon request from the corresponding author (<witschey@mail.med.upenn.edu>). All other data are within the paper. Introduction {#sec005}
============

There is increasing interest in quantitative, parametric mapping as a method for myocardial disease assessment in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Parametric maps provide detailed information about tissue properties that can be crucial for diagnosing and monitoring various cardiac conditions. Unlike traditional MRI techniques, which primarily offer anatomical images, parametric mapping quantifies physiological parameters such as T1 and T2 relaxation times, extracellular volume fraction, or myocardial strain. These quantitative measures enable a more nuanced understanding of the underlying pathology.

For instance, in cardiomyopathies like hypertrophic or dilated conditions, changes in tissue composition—such as fibrosis or edema—are often key factors influencing disease progression. Parametric mapping can detect these subtle alterations earlier and with greater specificity than conventional imaging methods. This is particularly important for assessing myocardial viability, guiding therapeutic interventions, and evaluating treatment efficacy over time.

Moreover, the non-invasive nature of MRI combined with parametric techniques offers a significant advantage in clinical settings where repeated assessments are necessary.

While many genes involved in these events have been identified, the genetic instructions must be translated into specific cellular responses (for example, mitosis, apoptosis, migration, adhesion) in order to shape and organize a complex 3-D structure like the urinary epithelium. While very little is known about the specific cellular behaviors that underlie epithelial morphogenesis in higher organisms, or how they are controlled, advances in genetic manipulation and imaging technologies now make it feasible to address such questions using mouse models. This project will focus on the cellular events that are regulated by the secreted factor GDNF, which signals through the Ret receptor tyrosine kinase. GDNF (Glial cell line-Derived Neurotrophic Factor) is a pivotal signaling molecule in kidney development, particularly influencing the formation and branching of ureteric buds from the Wolffian duct. Our approach will be to dissect how GDNF orchestrates specific cellular behaviors that are crucial for proper renal organogenesis.

Firstly, we aim to map out the spatiotemporal expression patterns of GDNF during kidney development using advanced imaging techniques such as confocal microscopy and in vivo live-cell imaging.

NEWS & EVENTS

Dordt College News

Learning takes a vacation in the Netherlands

The yearly Dordt alumni and friends “learning vacation” took on international proportions last May with its first tour through the Netherlands. “Other years, James Schaap and Robb De Haan have taken alumni and friends on a tour of the Great Plains. We wanted to do something similar,” said Professor Leendert van Beek, one of the tour hosts. The idea was to explore the roots of Dordt College’s heritage by visiting places significant to the Reformed Christian tradition, which has deep ties to Dutch history. The itinerary included stops at historic churches, museums, and universities that played a role in shaping the educational philosophies embraced by Dordt.

Participants began their journey in Amsterdam, where they visited the Rijksmuseum to learn about Dutch art and culture. From there, they traveled to Leiden, home to one of Europe's oldest universities, which has been influential in theological education.

base_text: The ability to quickly and cleanly sever tubular members, such as well casings that are deep underground, is an essential step during well maintenance and salvage operations. Typically, the industry relies on mechanical or explosive devices to perform such cutting. One type of explosive device, that is often used, is a shaped charge explosive cutter, which provides a simple, fast, and inexpensive method by which to sever pipes within a wellbore. During typical operations, shaped charge explosive cutters are lowered to a selected depth within a well, using a wireline, at which time they are detonated, producing pressure and/or molten materials that cut through the casing. A typical shaped charge tubular cutting device contains two circular layers of explosive material, each having a truncated cone shape.

base_text: With use of the inverse polymerase chain reaction technique to identify clonal forms of HIV, a clonal form of HIV was found in one of four KS cases. The HIV-positive tumor was an early KS lesion of the bowel, and uninvolved bowel from the same patient showed no clonal HIV. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrated the presence of prominent HIV-expressing macrophages that also coexpressed high levels of HIV tat, basic fibroblast growth factor, and interleukin-6. These data provide evidence for a pathogenic process termed "sequential neoplasia," wherein a clonal macrophage provides a growth factor milieu stimulating the proliferation of a responder cell population that ultimately becomes autonomous. In the current case, the macrophages expressing HIV were located adjacent to the KS tumor tissue and were found to be producing known KS growth factors. The absence of finding clonal HIV in three more advanced KS lesions suggests that the clonal macrophage may be required only for early pathogenesis and that sequential neoplastic changes occurring in the endothelial cells gave rise to autonomous KS. base_text:
Identification of a clonal form of HIV in early Kaposi's sarcoma: evidence for a novel model of oncogenesis, "sequential neoplasia". We recently reported clonal human immunodeficiency virus (HIV involvement in four acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS)-associated non-B-cell lymphoproliferations. In this study, we extended our investigation to Kaposi's sarcoma (KS), a common AIDS-associated malignancy. Our findings reveal that HIV can indeed play an active role not only as a co-factor but also potentially as a direct participant in the oncogenic process of KS through clonal integration events.

Using advanced molecular techniques such as single-cell PCR and next-generation sequencing, we identified specific clonal forms of HIV-1 integrated within the genomes of spindle cells derived from early-stage Kaposi's sarcoma lesions.

Q:

Very simple HTML / plain-text email not showing at all in microsoft live.com

I have been using a very simple PHP function to construct HTML / plain-text emails. It worked well in gmail and many other clients I saw. As it turns out, @live.com accounts don't get to see the email content at all. This issue likely stems from differences in how various email services handle multipart/alternative messages (which contain both HTML and plain text). 1. Introduction
===============

Cerebral palsy (CP) is a group of persistent central and posture developmental disorders, activity-restricted syndromes caused by non-progressive brain damage in developing fetuses or infants, ^\[[@R1],[@R2]\]^ Its main manifestations are central dyskinesia and abnormal posture. The prevalence of the disease is about 1.5%~0~∼4.0%~0~, affecting 2 per 1000 live births globally,[@R3] the incidence rate in China is about 1.8%~0~∼6.0%~0~. It presents a significant public health challenge due to its impact on individuals’ quality of life and their ability to participate fully in society. The condition arises from various prenatal, perinatal, or postnatal factors that result in brain injury at critical stages of development.

2. Etiology

The etiology of cerebral palsy is multifactorial, involving a complex interplay between genetic predispositions and environmental influences. During pregnancy, risk factors may include maternal infections such as rubella or cytomegalovirus, exposure to toxins like methylmercury, and metabolic conditions including gestational diabetes mellitus. Preterm birth and low birth weight are substantial contributors due to their association with underdeveloped fetal brain structures.

During the perinatal period, events that compromise oxygen supply to the infant's brain can lead to hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (HIE), a significant cause of CP.

Background
==========

The circular genome of *E. coli*is still an object of intense scientific research (see, e.g., \[[@B1]\]). It is a rich source of information on the organization of gene regulation, the interplay of different types of control exerted on gene expression, a model system for analyzing DNA topology, the model for which the most detailed electronically accessible transcriptional regulatory network has been compiled. Many processes, acting on a broad range of scales, contribute to the evolution of bacterial chromosomes. These include mutation and recombination at the molecular level, horizontal gene transfer between species, as well as more macroscopic events like large-scale genomic rearrangements and changes in genome size due to selective pressures from the environment. On a finer scale, the dynamic interplay between DNA topology and supercoiling plays a critical role in regulating access to genetic information, influencing processes such as replication initiation, transcriptional regulation, and chromosome segregation.

The *E. coli* chromosome is divided into several replicons, each with its own origin of replication (oriC) and unique timing for duplication during the cell cycle.

The long-term goal is to understand how calcium is used to control cell growth and differentiation, from signal perception, to gene expression, to changes in cell structure and physiology. The focus is on a novel family of calcium pumps identified in plants (ACAs). These pumps are most closely related to the plasma membrane calcium pumps in animals (PMCAs). A genetic disruption has been obtained for each of the 14 calcium pumps in the model plant Arabidopsis. This sets the stage for an exciting investigation into how these ACA and PMCA proteins orchestrate calcium dynamics within cells, impacting key cellular processes. By systematically comparing the phenotypes of each disrupted calcium pump mutant with wild-type plants, we can delineate their specific roles in development and stress responses.

Moreover, examining calcium signaling pathways involves detailed electrophysiological studies to understand the activation mechanisms of ACAs and their interactions with other cellular components such as receptors and ion channels.

This paper presents the first in a series of publications from the Census of the Local Universe experiment of tqhe Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF; @Bellm2019a [@Graham2019]).

History
After the collapse of Galaxy F.C., Brandon Murray and Chris Dawes decided to start a new club in Clarendon to take the parish' place at Jamaica's top tier and in 2000 Sporting Central Academy was formed. They play their home games at Murray's farm, a few miles down the road from May Pen. They finally clinched promotion to the top level in June 2007, under former managers Chris Dawes and Kevin Williams, and are the first team from Clarendon to compete there, since Hazard United's move to St Catherine in 2003. After Dawes left for the United States in 2009, Donovan Duckie took the reins but left the club in February 2010 after disagreements with Murray. Dawes would then return to manage the team until former assistant Vassell Reynolds was appointed head coach before the start of the 2011/2012 season.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides in vivo three-dimensional images with exquisite soft tissue contrast for investigating injured spinal cord (SC). In experimental studies with rat models of SCI, we regularly use MRI to follow the same animal longitudinally, so that each animal can serve as its own control. Conventional MRI, however, provides limited information on vascular changes that occur transiently following the injury. Novel imaging methods with capacity to sense vascular changes are required. base_text:
Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a major health and socio-economic problem, affecting many individuals. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides in vivo three-dimensional images with exquisite soft tissue contrast for investigating injured spinal cord (SC). In experimental studies with rat models of SCI, we regularly use MRI to follow the same animal longitudinally, so that each animal can serve as its own control. This approach significantly reduces variability and enhances our understanding of the injury's progression over time. By employing high-resolution imaging techniques such as diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and functional MRI (fMRI), we are able to assess not only structural changes but also alterations in neural connectivity and function.

One key advantage of using rat models is their well-characterized spinal cord anatomy, which allows for precise targeting during injury induction. This precision ensures consistent results across experiments, facilitating the comparison between different therapeutic interventions or genetic modifications aimed at promoting recovery.

Moreover, longitudinal MRI studies enable us to monitor the efficacy of various treatments in real-time. For instance, we can evaluate how stem cell therapies influence axonal regeneration and remyelination processes by observing changes in signal intensity and diffusion metrics over weeks or months post-injury.

Additionally, these imaging techniques allow for non-invasive monitoring of inflammation and edema dynamics within the injured spinal cord.

base_text:
Functional residual capacity in healthy preschool children. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the feasibility of routine functional residual capacity (FRC) measurements in healthy preschool children aged 2.7-6.4 yrs. Furthermore, accuracy and reproducibility were investigated and normal values were collected. A mass-produced closed-circuit helium dilution device (rolling seal) was used. One hundred thirty-five children participated, with the majority showing excellent tolerance for the procedure. The study found that FRC measurements were both feasible and safe in this age group. Significant effort was made to ensure the child's comfort and cooperation during the testing process, employing techniques such as parental presence and using engaging distractions.

Accuracy was confirmed through high correlation coefficients between repeated measures within individual subjects, demonstrating excellent reproducibility. Inter-tester reliability was also strong, suggesting that different operators could obtain consistent results. The normal values established in this study provide a useful reference for assessing respiratory function in preschool-aged children. These findings support the incorporation of FRC measurements into routine pediatric pulmonary evaluations and may aid in early detection of respiratory abnormalities.

import java.util. It looks like you're on the right track by focusing on `.hasNext()` and `.hasNextLine()`, as these are often involved in cases where infinite loops occur when reading from files or input streams.

The issue likely arises because of a misunderstanding of how these methods work. Both `.hasNext()` (for `Scanner` using tokens) and `.hasNextLine()` return true if there is another token/line available respectively. However, if you're not advancing the scanner by actually calling `.next()`, `.nextLine()`, or similar methods, you might be repeatedly checking for availability without consuming any input.

base_text:
Fungal Succession During the Decomposition of Ectomycorrhizal Fine Roots. Ectomycorrhizal (ECM) fine roots account for a substantial proportion of forest production and their decomposition releases large amounts of nutrients to the soil ecosystem. However, little is known about the fungi involved in ECM decomposition, including assemblages of fungal saprotrophs, endophytes, and the ECM fungi themselves. To address this gap, we conducted a study examining fungal succession during the decomposition of ECM fine roots over time. We collected root samples from various stages of decomposition in temperate forest ecosystems and analyzed them using high-throughput sequencing techniques to identify fungal communities.

Our results revealed a dynamic shift in fungal assemblages throughout the decomposition process. Initially, ECM fungi were dominant, reflecting their primary role in nutrient acquisition during the root's life. As decomposition progressed, saprotrophic fungi became increasingly prevalent, indicating their crucial role in breaking down complex organic matter and facilitating nutrient release.

base_text: Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a lesion suggestive of fibrocartilaginous embolic myelopathy with regional spinal cord edema, decreased disk signal intensity at L2-L3, and mild intervertebral disk protrusion at L1-L2 and L2-L3. Cerebral spinal fluid analysis showed no overt evidence of infection or neoplasia. Medical therapy was instituted, including corticosteroids and gastroprotectants as well as nursing care and physical therapy. The tiger began showing clinical improvement 2 wk after initiating treatment, progressing to the point where the animal was standing and intermittently walking. Three months after diagnosis, the tiger had regained muscle strength of its hind limbs and walked regularly with improving coordination. This case is the first report of antemortem diagnosis and successful medical management of suspected fibrocartilaginous embolic myelopathy in a large exotic felid.

Antemortem diagnosis and successful management of noncompressive segmental myelopathy in a Siberian-Bengal mixed breed tiger. A 10-yr-old female spayed mixed breed tiger presented for a 9-day history of acute and nonprogressive paralysis of the pelvic limbs. Physical examination revealed normal cranial nerve function, with no apparent deficits in mental status. The thoracic limb muscles were consistent with good tone and strength, while both pelvic limbs were completely flaccid, lacking proprioception, and devoid of nociceptive withdrawal reflexes. Initial neurological localization was centered on the spinal cord between T10 and L3.

Initial diagnostic imaging included radiographs and an MRI of the thoracolumbar spine. Radiographs showed mild vertebral bone density reduction but no obvious osseous lesions. MRI revealed a focal area of increased signal intensity on T2-weighted images at the level of T12-L1, consistent with myelopathy.

base_text:
Stable emulsions prepared by self-assembly of hyaluronic acid and chitosan for papain loading. A simple, green and effective process is developed to fabricate hyaluronic acid (HA)/chitosan (CS) complex colloidal particles through electrostatic interactions. Papain, a proteolytic enzyme with numerous industrial applications, was incorporated into these emulsions by exploiting the natural biocompatibility and non-toxic nature of both HA and CS. The process begins with dissolving hyaluronic acid in an acidic aqueous solution to ensure its solubility while maintaining structural integrity. Concurrently, chitosan is dissolved in a dilute acetic acid medium to promote protonation of amine groups, facilitating electrostatic attraction.

The subsequent step involves the gradual addition of the CS solution to the HA dispersion under constant stirring, promoting self-assembly through ionic interactions between negatively charged sulfate groups on HA and positively charged amino groups on CS.

base_text: Mechanisms that regulate progenitor cell quiescence and differentiation in slowly replacing tissues are not fully understood. Here, we demonstrate that the tumor suppressor p53 regulates both proliferation and differentiation of progenitors in the airway epithelium. p53 loss decreased ciliated cell differentiation and increased the self-renewal and proliferative capacity of club progenitors, increasing epithelial cell density. p53-deficient progenitors generated a pseudostratified epithelium containing basal-like cells in vitro and putative bronchioalveolar stem cells in vivo. Conversely, an additional copy of p53 increased quiescence and ciliated cell differentiation, highlighting the importance of tight regulation of p53 levels. Using single-cell RNA sequencing, we found that loss of p53 altered the molecular phenotype of progenitors and differentially modulated cell-cycle regulatory genes. Together, these findings reveal that p53 is an essential regulator of progenitor cell behavior, which shapes our understanding of stem cell quiescence during homeostasis and in cancer development.

p53 Regulates Progenitor Cell Quiescence and Differentiation in the Airway. Mechanisms that regulate progenitor cell quiescence and differentiation in slowly replacing tissues are not fully understood. The tumor suppressor protein p53 has been identified as a crucial regulator of these processes within the airway epithelium. Its role extends beyond its well-known function in preventing tumorigenesis, highlighting its importance in maintaining tissue homeostasis. Recent studies have shown that p53 influences both quiescence and differentiation of progenitor cells by modulating their response to environmental cues.

In quiescent states, p53 acts as a gatekeeper, ensuring that progenitor cells remain dormant until specific signals prompt their activation for tissue repair or regeneration. This is achieved through the regulation of various target genes involved in cell cycle arrest and apoptosis, thereby preventing unnecessary proliferation and potential damage accumulation. Furthermore, p53 influences the expression of factors like TGF-beta, which are pivotal in maintaining quiescence.

Upon activation, p53 facilitates differentiation by orchestrating a shift in progenitor cell fate decisions.

p53 Regulates Progenitor Cell Quiescence and Differentiation in the Airway. Mechanisms that regulate progenitor cell quiescence and differentiation in slowly replacing tissues are not fully understood. The tumor suppressor protein p53 has been identified as a crucial regulator of these processes within the airway epithelium. Its role extends beyond its well-known function in preventing tumorigenesis, highlighting its importance in maintaining tissue homeostasis. Recent studies have shown that p53 influences both quiescence and differentiation of progenitor cells by modulating their response to environmental cues.

In quiescent states, p53 acts as a gatekeeper, ensuring that progenitor cells remain dormant until specific signals prompt their activation for tissue repair or regeneration. This is achieved through the regulation of various target genes involved in cell cycle arrest and apoptosis, thereby preventing unnecessary proliferation and potential damage accumulation. Furthermore, p53 influences the expression of factors like TGF-beta, which are pivotal in maintaining quiescence.

Upon activation, p53 facilitates differentiation by orchestrating a shift in progenitor cell fate decisions.

base_text: The most common type of systemic amyloidosis (primary amyloidosis or AL amyloidosis) is caused by production of immunoglobulin light chain or light chain fragment secondary to an underlying plasma cell dyscrasia. Other systemic amyloidoses (secondary amyloidoses) include AA amyloid (caused as a result of chronic inflammatory disease), ATTR caused by alteration in transthyretin (TTR) protein because of one of several mutations (familial amyloid) or misfolding of wild-type transthyretin protein (senile amyloidosis), and dialysis-related amyloidosis (deposition of beta 2 microglobulin). Primary amyloidosis is the only type of systemic amyloidosis that responds to cytotoxic chemotherapy directed against the abnormal plasma cell clone, the source of amyloidogenic light chains. Other treatment strategies to prevent amyloid formation by altering the equilibrium between soluble precursor and insoluble fibrils, destabilizing the amyloid fibril protein, or antibodies directed against amyloid fibrils are being investigated and show promise but are not available for clinical use \[[@B3]--[@B5]\]. This paper will focus on the role of autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for primary systemic amyloidosis. 2.

base_text:
1. Introduction {#sec1}
===============

Amyloidosis is a disease of protein misfolding in which the involved protein acquires an abnormal beta-pleated sheet configuration rather than the native alpha helical state \[[@B1]\]. The amyloid protein is insoluble, and its deposition in various tissues causes tissue damage and organ dysfunction. The hallmark feature of amyloidosis lies in these fibrillar aggregates that stain positively with specific dyes like Congo red or thioflavin T under polarized light due to their unique structural properties \[[@B2]\]. Amyloids can accumulate locally, forming deposits within a single organ such as the liver (hepatic amyloid), heart (cardiac amyloidosis), or kidneys (renal amyloidosis). Alternatively, they may disseminate systemically throughout multiple organs, leading to generalized forms of the disease. 

The clinical manifestations are highly variable and depend on the type of protein involved in forming the fibrils as well as which tissues accumulate these deposits \[[@B3]\]. For instance, light chain (AL) amyloidosis typically affects peripheral nerves, heart muscle, kidneys, liver, spleen, and gastrointestinal tract.

base_text:
**To the Editor:** Although most human enterovirus (EV) (genus *Enterovirus*, family *Picornaviridae*) infections are asymptomatic, they can cause upper respiratory illness, febrile rash, aseptic meningitis, pleurodynia, encephalitis, acute flaccid paralysis, and neonatal sepsislike disease ([@R1]). Most EVs have been implicated in aseptic meningitis, most notably echovirus (E) 30, 9, 6, and 11 and coxsackie B virus (CBV) type 5 ([@R2]); other serotypes are less frequently associated with neurologic disease. New EV serotypes have come to light, chiefly as a result of molecular typing methods ([@R3]*--*[@R6]). These advancements in detection and classification have significantly expanded our understanding of the epidemiology and pathogenic potential of enteroviruses. For instance, human parechovirus (HPeV), previously considered non-pathogenic, has been increasingly recognized as a cause of severe neurological disease ([@R7]). Similarly, EV-A71 and CV-A16 are now well-documented causes of hand-foot-and-mouth disease with occasional progression to more serious conditions such as acute flaccid paralysis or encephalitis ([@R8]).

The emergence of these new serotypes underscores the dynamic nature of enterovirus evolution and transmission. Molecular typing has not only facilitated accurate identification but also helped trace outbreaks back to their sources, thereby improving public health responses ([@R9]). This is particularly crucial in settings where co-infections with other pathogens may complicate clinical presentations.

Moreover, understanding the genetic diversity among EV serotypes aids vaccine development efforts.

base_text: Epidemiology of triglycerides: a view from Framingham. New analyses from the Framingham Heart Study are presented showing that men and women who have high triglyceride levels (> 1.7 mmol/liter) and a low high-density lipoprotein (HDL) level (< 1.03 mmol/liter) run a significantly higher rate of coronary artery disease and can be identified, and that this risk group (high triglyceride-low HDL) is, independently of the major risk factors (including low HDL), related to occurrence of coronary artery disease. Further, this trait appears to be common in our society, producing twice as many cases of coronary artery disease during the 14 years of the Framingham study as the next highest disease-producing lipid abnormality. This trait of high triglyceride-low HDL is associated in the medical literature with increased insulin resistance, higher blood sugars (within the normal range), higher uric acid levels, hypertension, and centrally mediated obesity. Because total cholesterol in people with these traits may be less than or just slightly greater than 5.2 mmol/liter, they are missed or neglected by most cholesterol screening programs.

Epidemiology of triglycerides: a view from Framingham. New analyses from the Framingham Heart Study are presented showing that men and women who have high triglyceride levels (> 1.7 mmol/liter) and a low high-density lipoprotein (HDL) level (< 1.03 mmol/liter) run a significantly higher rate of coronary artery disease and can be identified, and that this risk group (high triglyceride-low HDL) is, independently of the major risk factors (including low HDL), related to occurrence of coronary artery disease. Further, this trait appears to be common in our society, producing twice as many cases of coronary artery disease during the 14 years of the Framingham study as the next highest disease-producing lipid abnormality. This trait of high triglyceride-low HDL is associated in the medical literature with increased insulin resistance, higher blood sugars (within the normal range), higher uric acid levels, hypertension, and centrally mediated obesity. These findings underscore the importance of considering this specific combination of lipid abnormalities as a significant risk factor for coronary artery disease beyond traditional metrics like total cholesterol or low-density lipoprotein (LDL) levels alone.

The Framingham Heart Study's comprehensive data collection over several decades provides valuable insights into how triglycerides and HDL interact in contributing to cardiovascular health risks. By focusing on the high triglyceride-low HDl phenotype, researchers can better understand its role as a marker for metabolic syndrome components that contribute independently to coronary artery disease.
